title: About Jack
text: Hello! I’m Jack! Welcome to my blog with some interesting facts and trends inside of the sports world! Here is where I will be posting different trends inside the sports world especially inside of NCAA Basketball!

title: March Madness Isn’t Random. We Just Seed It That Way
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggalt)
library(gt)
library(ggbeeswarm)
library(ggrepel)


Every March, millions of brackets get busted within days. Upsets, buzzer beaters, and Cinderella runs make the NCAA Tournament feel completely unpredictable. But what if it’s not? What if the teams that win are actually telling us who they are long before the tournament starts?
This project looks at whether NCAA Tournament success is truly random, or if the numbers do a better job predicting winners than seeding alone.
To start, let’s look at the last four tournaments and ask a simple question: In the 2024 NCAA tournament did being a high seed guarantee a successful tournament?


Code
MarchMadness2024 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2024.csv")
MarchMadness2023 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2023.csv")
MarchMadness2022 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2022.csv")
MarchMadness2021 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2021.csv")

Final_data <- bind_rows(
  MarchMadness2024,
  MarchMadness2023,
  MarchMadness2022,
  MarchMadness2021
)
seed_summary <- MarchMadness2024 |>
  filter(!is.na(seed)) |>
  group_by(seed) |>
  summarize(
    max_wins = max(tournament_wins),
    avg_wins = mean(tournament_wins)
  )

seed_summary |>
  rename(
    Seed = seed,
    `Max Wins` = max_wins,
    `Avg Wins` = avg_wins
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Tournament Wins by Seed",
    subtitle = "Higher seeds win more, but how much more? A breakdown of min, max, and average wins."
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data from 2024"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = c(Seed))
  ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_borders(
      sides = c("top", "bottom"),
      color = "grey",
      weight = px(1)
    ),
    locations = cells_column_labels(everything())
  )








Tournament Wins by Seed


Higher seeds win more, but how much more? A breakdown of min, max, and average wins.

By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data from 2024








The table above makes one thing clear and thats seeding is not a guarantee of anything. Every seed from 1 through 10 has produced teams that lost immediately and teams that made deep runs. For example, the 2023 - 2024 Clemson team made it all the way to the Elite 8 as a 6 seed. Even the top seeds, which carry the highest expectations, still show a wide range of outcomes from year to year. But looking at individual extremes can be misleading.
To get a clearer picture, it helps to zoom out and look at averages from 2021 - 2024. Does seeding still matter when you look at the bigger pattern across all four tournaments?


Code
seed_avg <- Final_data |>
  group_by(seed) |>
  filter(!is.na(seed)) |>
  summarise(avg_wins = mean(tournament_wins))

ggplot(seed_avg, aes(x = factor(seed), y = avg_wins)) +
  geom_col(fill = "Steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Higher Seeds Usually Win More Games. But Not Always.",
    subtitle = "Average tournament wins by seed, 2021–2024",
    x = "Tournament Seed",
    y = "Average Tournament Wins",
    caption = "By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data, 2021–2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









On average, higher seeds do win more games. The trend is real. But averages can hide a lot of noise, and this one does. A seed’s average win total tells you where most teams end up, but it doesn’t tell you how wide the range of outcomes actually is.
A 1 seed that loses in the first round counts the same as one that wins the national championship when you’re computing an average. Chart 3 pulls that curtain back by showing the full spread of wins within each seed, making it easier to see just how unpredictable March Madness remains even for the best teams in the country.


Code
outliers <- Final_data |>
  filter(seed <= 12) |>
  group_by(seed) |>
  slice_max(tournament_wins, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE)

Final_data |>
  filter(seed <= 12) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(seed), y = tournament_wins)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "blue", alpha = 0.5, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_point(data = outliers, color = "red", size = 2) +
  geom_text_repel(data = outliers, aes(label = team), size = 2.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:6, labels = c("0", "1", "2 (Sweet 16)", "3", "4 (Final Four)", "5", "6 (Champion)")) +
  labs(
    title = "Same Seed, Very Different Outcomes",
    subtitle = "The full spread of wins per seed reveals how unpredictable March Madness is even for the best teams",
    x = "Tournament Seed",
    y = "Tournament Wins",
    caption = "By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data, 2021-2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


Warning: Removed 6 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_text_repel()`).









Even after looking at averages and distributions, one question keeps coming up: if seeding can’t fully explain what happens in March, what can? Seeds reflect regular season performance and committee judgment, but they don’t directly measure how good a team actually is on the court.
UConn is the perfect example. The Huskies won back-to-back national championships in 2023 and 2024, going a combined 12-0 in tournament play. Their dominance wasn’t about where the committee slotted them it was about how prepared they were when it mattered most.
And they aren’t alone. An 8 seed North Carolina squad reached the national championship game in 2022, while multiple 11 seeds (Like Loyola Chicago in 2018) racked up four and five wins in a single run. These aren’t flukes. They suggest that strength of schedule, defensive efficiency, and tournament experience drive outcomes in ways a seed number can’t capture. That’s exactly what the next chart digs into.
That’s where advanced efficiency metrics come in. KenPom’s adjusted efficiency margin measures how much better or worse a team scores relative to its opponents, adjusted for the quality of competition. If anything predicts tournament success, it should be that.

title: Can KenPom Predict March Madness Contenders?
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)


── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors


Code
MarchMadness2024 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2024.csv")
MarchMadness2023 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2023.csv")
MarchMadness2022 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2022.csv")
MarchMadness2021 <- read.csv("data/final_march_madness_2021.csv")

Final_data <- bind_rows(
  MarchMadness2024,
  MarchMadness2023,
  MarchMadness2022,
  MarchMadness2021
)


March Madness always feels unpredictable. Every year there are upsets, busted brackets, and teams that seem to come out of nowhere. It’s easy to think the tournament is completely random. But is it really? Instead of focusing on seeding, this analysis looks at KenPom efficiency metrics to see if the best teams on paper are actually the ones winning games and making deep runs.
The first chart looks at the relationship between a team’s adjusted efficiency margin and how many games it wins in the tournament. Even though the results are somewhat scattered, there is still a clear trend. Teams with stronger efficiency margins tend to win more games, while teams with lower ratings are more likely to lose early.
This suggests that success in March isn’t completely random. Better teams, at least according to KenPom, have a real advantage when the tournament begins.


Code
eff_groups <- Final_data |>
  filter(!is.na(adjem), !is.na(tournament_wins)) |>
  mutate(group = "Teams w/ a ADJEM of 24 or Lower") |>
  mutate(group = replace(group, adjem >= 25, "Team's w/ a ADJEM of 25 or Higher"))

eff_summary <- eff_groups |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarize(avg_wins = mean(tournament_wins))

eff_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = group, y = avg_wins)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Better teams win more games in March",
    subtitle = "Teams with stronger KenPom ratings consistently win more tournament games",
    x = "Team ADJEM",
    y = "Average tournament wins",
    caption = "By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data, 2021-2024"
  ) +
   theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









This suggests that success in March isn’t completely random. Better teams, at least according to KenPom, have a real advantage when the tournament begins.
Chart 2 makes the trend clearer, but there’s still one more question to answer. Instead of looking at averages, it helps to flip the perspective and look at the teams that actually went the furthest.


Code
champions <- Final_data |>
  filter(
    Team == "Baylor" |
    Team == "Kansas" |
    Team == "Connecticut"
  )

Final_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = RankAdjOE, y = RankAdjDE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = .4, size = 2) +
  geom_point(data = champions, color = "red", size = 3) +
  geom_text(
    data = champions,
    aes(label = Team),
    size = 3.5,
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 1.2
  ) +
  scale_x_reverse() +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  labs(
    title = "Championship teams are elite on both ends of the court",
    subtitle = "Recent champions rank near the top in both offense and defense",
    x = "Adjusted offensive rank (lower is better)",
    y = "Adjusted defensive rank (lower is better)",
    caption = "By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data, 2021-2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 6),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


Warning: Removed 2095 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









March Madness will always have surprises, but it is not completely random. The data shows that better teams, based on KenPom ratings, tend to win more games and make deeper runs. While there will always be upsets, these numbers do a good job of identifying real contenders before the tournament even begins.


Code
champions <- Final_data |>
  filter(
    Team == "Baylor" |
    Team == "Kansas" |
    Team == "Connecticut"
  )

Final_data |>
  ggplot(aes(x = RankAdjOE, y = RankAdjDE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = .4, size = 2) +
  geom_point(data = champions, color = "red", size = 3) +
  geom_text(
    data = champions,
    aes(label = Team),
    size = 3.5,
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 1.2
  ) +
  scale_x_reverse() +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  labs(
    title = "Championship teams are elite on both ends of the court",
    subtitle = "Recent champions rank near the top in both offense and defense",
    x = "Adjusted offensive rank (lower is better)",
    y = "Adjusted defensive rank (lower is better)",
    caption = "By Jack Kohout | Source: KenPom and NCAA Tournament data, 2021-2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 6),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


Warning: Removed 2095 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
